This isn't a reasonable increase because I frequently need to do cargo build --release during development.

If someone wanted this then I'd be open to adding it only in CI when the final release artifact is produced.

Thanks for working on this! Unfortunately, I think there was a miscommunication or I didn't make myself sufficiently clear, and as a result, this PR isn't quite what I was expecting.

First and foremost, these docs need to be our reference point: https://docs.rs/ignore/0.3.1/ignore/struct.WalkBuilder.html#ignore-rules --- I'll speak in terms of those.

A critical aspect of ignore file checking is that there are layers of ignore rules. Notice that in the second bullet item, each class of ignore file has its own precedence rules. This is necessary not only for predictable UX, but it also matches how `git` does things. (`git` has its own layers: `.gitignore`, `.git/info/exclude` and global gitignores.) In particular, *any* `.gitignore` file---regardless of where it lives---will override *all* `.git/info/exclude` files.

This precedence hierarchy continues with `.ignore`. This is best shown with an example:

```
$ mkdir /tmp/demorules
$ cd /tmp/demorules
$ git init
$ mkdir foo
$ echo test > baz
$ echo test > foo/quux
$ echo test > foo/bar
$ echo -e 'baz\nquux' > .gitignore
$ echo '!quux' > foo/.gitignore
```

If you stop here, you'll notice that `git` won't ignore `quux` since the `.gitignore` inside the `foo` directory overrides the rules found in the `.gitignore` in the root of the repository. If you run `rg --files`, then ripgrep will come up with the same results.

However, if you run

```
$ echo quux > .ignore
```

in the root of the repository and run `rg --files`, you'll notice that `quux` is actually ignored, even though a `.gitignore` file exists that is "closer" to `quux` in the `foo` directory. This is because *any* `.ignore` overrides *all* `.gitignore` files.

So where am I going with this? Well, when I said this:

> I think the way to solve your problem is to add an additional layer of ignore files that has higher precedence than .ignore but uses a custom (probably application specific) name.

I meant that the application specific ignore files should actually be another layer, in addition to `.ignore`. That means *any* application specific ignore file (like `.fdignore`) will override *all* `.ignore` rules, just like *any* `.ignore` file will override *all* `.gitignore` rules.

In this PR, you've implemented such that `.ignore` is inter-mingled with the application specific ignores. That is, a `foo/.fdignore` won't override a `foo/bar/.ignore`, even though I think it should in order to be consistent with how the ignore rules are processed today.

Doing this is a little harder, but you should still mostly be able to work off of what's there. Please ping me with questions!

One major thing missing from this PR is tests, and we really need those before merging this. The tests should cover at least the following:

1. Basic functionality. i.e., Can I specify a custom ignore file and have it be respected?
2. Precedence. Precedence should be checked between different ignore file types and also between different custom ignore names.
3. Integration tests for ripgrep itself. These tests don't need to have as much coverage as 1+2, but something that checks that it actually works is fine.

I think 1+2 should be in the `ignore` crate while 3 should be in `tests/tests.rs`.

My main concern here is supporting arguments like `06` to mean "left pad, using zeroes." Right now, in this PR, I believe `06` will be parsed as `6`, which we will left pad with spaces. I am fine with starting there, but I very much expect someone to come along and request zero padding as well. At that point, if we change `06` to left pad zeroes, then it would technically be a breaking change, which I'd like to avoid.

As a simple work-around to this, I'd suggest return an error if the number starts with a `0`.

If and when we add a custom format character, we shouldn't use some other library to do left padding for us. Re-implementing this specific part of the `format!` macro should be a very short helper function.

Thanks for working on this! Unfortunately, I think a lot more work needs to be put into this.

First and foremost, we need a specification for how this works, including its interaction with other flags. The interaction with other flags can be complicated, but it needs thought through. It is possible that some of the answer there is "we don't support it yet, or won't support it, so present an error." The benefit of an error is that we can change it in the future to not be an error. The downside of an error is that it's bad UX.

Secondly, #244 describes a number of concerns I have and even discusses a possible format. In particular, the "ackmate" format is something that other tools already know how to read, presumably.

I think the next step here is to go back to #244 and write up the documentation for this feature first. That way, we can discuss the semantics of this before looking at the code.

Awesome! I ended up merging this with your other PR. It makes the trait definitions a bit more gnarly, but the idea still works. I also removed the trait object from the signature of `visit` and instead just used normal parametric polymorphism. Although, now that I say that, I wonder if a trait object is preferrable. There's unlikely any performance benefit, and a trait object would avoid copying all of the traversal code for each implementation of `ParallelVisitorBuilder`.

I saw your ideas with respect to reduce APIs, but I think I'd rather stick with the smallest addition that unblocks folks. As I've said a few other things, IMO, the API of `ignore` is an unmitigated disaster and needs to be completely rethought anyway. So until then, I'm somewhat okay with continuing to make the API worse as long as it serves a real need while also minimizing churn. So I really appreciated that this change is backwards compatible. :-)

So one thing that I'm concerned about here is the abrupt switchover to asciidoctor. I think what I'd rather do is to use asciidoctor like you have in this PR, but fallback to a2x if asciidoctor isn't available. That will make it easy to release this change in a point release with a note that `a2x` support will be removed and man page generation will require asciidoctor in ripgrep 13. That will give package maintainers a bit more slack to deal with this change.

I know it's more of a pain to do that here. Let me know how you'd like to proceed. If you don't do it, then I'd be happy to take over this PR and do it myself, but I don't know when that will be. If you do want to do it, then I'd recommend just having two totally different paths between asciidoc and asciidoctor. No need to try to reuse code between them in `build.rs`. Then we can just lift out `a2x` when we're ready.

Your hesitation about the use of `warn!` is spot on. I suspect we need to find a way to at least make it possible for the error to be returned to the caller. Log messages like `warn!` aren't really something I've used much in ripgrep, as I see them more for developer usage. But the kinds of errors reported here are most definitely of interest to the user. Additionally, these are messages that ripgrep needs to be able to squash if requested (i.e., `--no-messages`).

More concretely, I _think_ what's needed here is this:

1. Move the reap logic to its own separate routine and make that routine idempotent.
2. Call that reap logic from within the `read` routine, as was done before.
3. Also call it in `Drop` as a last-ditch-effort. Retaining the `warn!` here in case of an error is fine. (Because of steps 4 and 5.)
4. Add a `close` method to the public API of `CommandReader` that permits callers to stop and reap the process early.
5. Have ripgrep call `close` whenever it uses a `CommandReader`. (This looks doable inside of `search_preprocessor` in `crates/core/search.rs`.)

Since ripgrep will always call `close`, it also follows that the `warn!` messages will never appear. So if they do appear, they can be considered a bug.

And on top of that, this behavior should be documented in the `grep-cli` crate. Probably best to put it on the docs for the new `close` method.

Nice work but yeah, there is a fair bit of work left here. The two big items:

1. It is a hard requirement that if `--sort` is not given, then there should be no additional overhead here. But in this PR, it looks like you always collect all of the paths before starting a search. Today, ripgrep will start searching well before it finishes directory traversal.
2. There are a number of places where errors are being swallowed. We can't do that.

I'm also not sure about moving sorting to the `main` functions. I think it's probably fine, although I was trying to keep the `main` functions as simple as I could. But if we are going to deal with sorting in `main.rs`, then I think it would be nice to add a short comment in the parallel searcher stating why it doesn't handle sorting. (Because if sorting is enabled, then parallel searching is disabled in `args.rs`.)

Nice, thank you! This fixes quite a gnarly bug that I've seen a lot of folks run into for a while. Thank you.

I ended up fixing two problems with this patch though:

1. First, the optimization does not look correct to me. The issue is that, for some ignore files, the existence of any rule regardless of where it is in the directory tree will override any other rule from a different type of ignore file. For example, `.rgignore` has higher precedent than `.ignore`. So you need to let all of the rules be visited. In practice, I doubt this optimization matters too much anyway.
2. This used `Path::strip_prefix` instead of ripgrep's own `strip_prefix` routine which does things a little differently. This meant that giving ripgrep a path like `.////path` would still trigger the bug. But using ripgrep's `strip_prefix` routine avoids this issue.

Overall this still feels Not Right to me, but it fixes a bug and doesn't seem to cause any other regressions. Hopefully we can straighten out this logic in a better way in the future when I overhaul the `ignore` crate.

In this case I guess then it makes sense to have a custom provider.

I have some thoughts about design from my own experience.

This implementation creates a division between Editable and Read Only schemas. They are now either one or the other. I'm wondering if we can make it more flexible to consider a mixture of static and dynamic content. 

In our case, we still work from a fork where we allow users to type in a mixture of static text and dynamic, e.g.:

```
Hi {{name}}, we hope you like your order!
```

In this fork we have an attribute named `schema.content` which contains the above text and we pre-process this to substitute values into the placeholders. We have some modifications to the editor code to use `content` rather than `value`, similar to how you have used `readOnlyValue`

I wonder if an attribute name such as `content` might be more generic and flexible for supporting some input content that is separate from the `value` for other plugin developers. 

I'm also wondering from a UX experience if there should be different schemas just because it's Read Only.  It might be clearer to have this as an option that you can toggle in the details panel. Or it might not, maybe it needs to be tried 🤔 


Anyway, these are just ideas. This PR looks great. I'm just starting work on my plugin now so I might suggest an iteration in future :)

Line range hint `21-83`: **Potential “SingleOrDefault” Edge**  
Several properties (e.g., `MockBehaviorStrict`, `MockBehaviorLoose`, `MockBehaviorDefault`) rely on `SingleOrDefault()`. While typically only one field exists for each in the enum, if the compilation environment has partial or unexpected references to Moq (rare or malicious scenario), there could be more than one matching symbol. If that happens, `SingleOrDefault()` will throw.  


**Why it’s a concern**  
In unusual builds or reflection-based merges, multiple “Strict” fields might exist, causing the analyzer to crash instead of degrade gracefully.

**Example**  
Any scenario in a multi-targeted or multi-assembly environment that redefines `MockBehavior` or merges metadata incorrectly.

**Proposed fallback**  
Fallback gracefully if multiple matches are found. For example:
```diff
    internal IFieldSymbol? MockBehaviorStrict => 
-       MockBehavior?.GetMembers("Strict").OfType<IFieldSymbol>().SingleOrDefault();
+       MockBehavior?.GetMembers("Strict").OfType<IFieldSymbol>()
+           .Where(symbol => !symbol.IsImplicitlyDeclared)
+           .Take(1)
+           .SingleOrDefault();
```
This ensures we only grab the first valid match, avoiding a hard exception if duplicates appear.

I frankly do not understand the intent of this contribution at all.

This project is a recipe. The entire build process is encapsulated in a single, simple command: `docker build . -t cook`. All contributors use this method and are expected to solve their own environment-specific issues. Your scripts are a workaround for a problem that only you seem to have.

Imagine the hundreds of Linux distributions in the world, each with a different package manager. It is impossible to guarantee that your script would work perfectly for everyone, in every country. **If you cannot commit to something that works universally, you should not be adding it to the repository in the first place**. This introduces a massive maintenance burden.

For instance, you've hardcoded Huawei mirrors. How is a developer in the US supposed to build this? You've also introduced a slew of Debian-specific dependencies, for which I see no justification. These are not universal problems.

This is an **extremely simple** MkDocs project. If you don't know how to build an MkDocs site, that is an issue with MkDocs, not this project. If you find the `docker build` command too hard to remember, the appropriate solution is a `Makefile`, which is precisely what that tool is for.

The entire point of Docker is to solve environment discrepancies. Your contribution re-introduces them. This project is so simple that locking down dependencies to this extent is completely unnecessary.

I cannot comprehend the purpose of this pull request. Do not merge this.

```cpp
explicit QuantumRegister(size_t num_qubits)
    : num_qubits(num_qubits), state_vector(1ULL << num_qubits) {

}
```

- No support for arbitrary state initialization

```cpp
    const double epsilon = 1e-10;
    // ...
}
```

3. Phase Rotation:
```cpp

```
- Limited to two-qubit operations
- No optimization for sparse state vectors
- Missing common quantum gates

## Build Integration

1. CMake Configuration:
```cmake
lib(math)
```
- Minimal CMake integration
- No dependency management
- Missing version requirements
- No platform-specific optimizations

1. Testing Requirements:

- Add performance benchmarks
- Implement integration tests

2. Implementation Completeness:
- Complete essential quantum operations
- Add measurement capabilities
- Implement common quantum gates

- Include quantum operation specifications

- Enhance CMake configuration
- Add proper dependency management
- Include platform optimizations
- Add version requirements

## Additional Notes
- PR scope differs significantly from description (MSVC flag vs quantum implementation)
- Implementation appears to be work-in-progress
- Core quantum computing features missing
- Integration with math framework unclear

Please address these issues before proceeding with the PR review. The implementation requires significant work to meet production standards.

   - Add test coverage specifically for Sum::sq() optimization cases
   - Ensure Integer comparison logic remains independent of Sum optimization states
   - Document the optimization dependencies between Sum and Integer classes

`163-170`: **Structs/enums still raise diagnostics – add a universal value-type guard** `mockedType.IsSealed && mockedType.TypeKind != TypeKind.Delegate` evaluates to `true` for *every* value-type because all structs/enums are sealed. Apply an early return for delegates *and* all value-types, then check `IsSealed` only for reference types: ```diff private static bool ShouldReportDiagnostic(ITypeSymbol mockedType) { - // Check if the mocked type is sealed (but allow delegates) - // Note: All delegates in .NET are sealed by default, but they can still be mocked by Moq - - // For nullable value types (Nullable), skip diagnostic - if (mockedType.OriginalDefinition?.SpecialType == SpecialType.System_Nullable_T) - { - return false; - } - - // For reference types, ignore nullable annotation; report if sealed and not a delegate - return mockedType.IsSealed && mockedType.TypeKind != TypeKind.Delegate; + // Delegates are allowed even though they are sealed. + if (mockedType.TypeKind == TypeKind.Delegate) + { + return false; + } + + // Skip *all* value-types (structs, enums, Nullable). + if (mockedType.IsValueType) + { + return false; + } + + // At this point we have a reference type; report only if it's sealed. + return mockedType.IsSealed; } ``` This removes the false positives highlighted in previous reviews and aligns the code with the XML comment.

## Pull Request Description: Implement Mock.Get() Literal Validation Analyzer (Moq1301)

This PR addresses legacy TODO items by implementing a new analyzer and confirming coverage for others. The main change is the introduction of a new analyzer rule (Moq1301) that warns when `Mock.Get()` is called with literal values, which is not a valid usage pattern in Moq.

### Summary of Changes
- **New Analyzer:**
  - Added `MockGetShouldNotTakeLiteralsAnalyzer` (Moq1301) to detect and warn when `Mock.Get()` is called with literals (e.g., strings, numbers, null, or default values).
  - Updated `MoqKnownSymbols` to support detection of `Mock.Get` method symbols.
  - Registered the new rule in `AnalyzerReleases.Unshipped.md` and `DiagnosticIds.cs`.
- **Documentation:**
  - Added `docs/rules/Moq1301.md` with usage examples, suppression instructions, and rationale.
  - Updated rule listings in `README.md` and `docs/rules/README.md` to include Moq1301.
- **Tests:**
  - Added comprehensive tests in `MockGetShouldNotTakeLiteralsAnalyzerTests.cs` to cover all flagged and valid usage patterns.

### Legacy TODO Items Review
- **Setup() should be used for methods, not for properties:** Already implemented as Moq1101.
- **Setup() requires overridable members:** Already implemented as Moq1200.
- **Mock.Get() should not take literals:** Implemented as Moq1301 in this PR.
- **AdvancedMatcherAttribute should accept only IMatcher types:** Not feasible (types do not exist in current Moq versions).
- **Checks for protected mocks when dynamically referencing their members:** Not feasible (C# compiler already prevents this).

### Example
```csharp
// ❌ These will be flagged by Moq1301
Mock.Get<string>("literal string");
Mock.Get<ISampleInterface>(null);
Mock.Get<ISampleInterface>(default(ISampleInterface));

// ✅ Correct usage
var mockObject = new Mock<ISampleInterface>().Object;
Mock.Get(mockObject);
```

### Rationale
Calling `Mock.Get()` with a literal value is always incorrect, as only objects created by Moq can be associated with a mock. This analyzer prevents subtle bugs and enforces correct usage.

.github/workflows/main.yml (2) `4-15`: **Boolean inputs: prefer explicit string casting to avoid silent type coercion** GitHub will pass unchecked values from the UI as *strings* (`'true'`, `'false'`), yet the later `if:` expressions compare against the *boolean* literal `true`. This mismatch is benign for `workflow_dispatch` events (it comes through as a string and you explicitly compare to `'true'` later), but for scheduled runs `github.event.inputs.*` is **undefined** which becomes the empty string – not the literal `false`. Consider normalising at the source to guarantee predictable values. ```diff - force_baseline: + force_baseline: ... - default: false + default: 'false' ``` …and then always compare with string equality in downstream conditionals. This prevents edge-cases where the expression engine treats an empty value as truthy/falsy in surprising ways. --- `168-181`: **Step only works on PowerShell-enabled runners** `pwsh` is fine under Windows and the cross-platform dotnet image on Linux, but on macOS the pre-installed PowerShell may not exist or be outdated. Either: 1. Use the `pwsh` action: `shell: pwsh {0}` which installs PowerShell; or 2. Convert to a portable Bash snippet. Failure to do so will abort the macOS-matrix leg.

`40-42`: **Unknown runner label `windows-11-arm`** Only self-hosted runners can use arbitrary labels. GitHub-hosted images expose `windows-latest`, `windows-2022` etc., but no official `windows-11-arm`. Action-lint flags this. Replace with either a supported label (`windows-2022`) or configure a self-hosted runner and add it to `actionlint.yaml`. --- `207-213`: **Potential cache corruption when `FORCE_BASELINE` is true** Using the same key while forcing a refresh can overwrite a good cache if the benchmark step later fails. Safest approach: generate a fresh key when `FORCE_BASELINE` is requested. ```diff -key: baseline-performance-${{ matrix.os }}-${{ steps.get-baseline-sha.outputs.sha }} +key: baseline-performance-${{ matrix.os }}-${{ steps.get-baseline-sha.outputs.sha }}${{ env.FORCE_BASELINE == 'true' && format('-{0}', github.run_id) || '' }} ```

**📄 CodeRabbit Inference Engine (CONTRIBUTING.md)**

> `tests/**/*.cs`: Run all unit tests using 'dotnet test --settings ./build/targets/tests/test.runsettings'. All tests must pass.
> Every analyzer and fixer must have comprehensive unit tests.
> Use the project's data-driven test pattern for code fix tests: provide a public static IEnumerable<object[]> data source, use [Theory] and [MemberData], and define a static Template function with placeholders.
> In code fix tests, the code that triggers the analyzer MUST be wrapped with the diagnostic marker syntax (e.g., {|Moq1210:...|}).
> Use AllAnalyzersVerifier for tests that verify code patterns don't trigger unwanted diagnostics from any Moq analyzer.
> Group analyzer tests by Moq version compatibility: place tests for features only available in Moq 4.18.4+ in a 'new' group, and tests for features available in both 4.8.2 and 4.18.4 in a 'both' or 'old' group.
> Do not include tests for APIs or patterns that do not exist in the targeted Moq version; such tests will fail at compile time.
> Only set up or verify virtual, abstract, or interface members in Moq analyzer tests. Do not attempt to set up or verify non-virtual, static, or sealed members.
> Use SetupAdd and SetupRemove only for virtual events, and only in Moq 4.18.4+.
> Setups for explicit interface implementations must use the correct cast syntax (e.g., ((IMyInterface)x).Method()).
> Use .Protected().Setup(...) only for protected virtual members, and only in Moq 4.18.4+.
> Do not add analyzer tests for static, const, or readonly members, as Moq cannot mock or set up these members.
> Always include both Task and ValueTask scenarios in analyzer tests for async methods.
> If your analyzer or code fix interacts with Callback or SetupSequence, ensure you have tests for both single and sequence setups.
> Always specify and test for both MockBehavior.Default and MockBehavior.Strict where relevant in analyzer tests.
> If mocking internal members, ensure [assembly: InternalsVisibleTo("DynamicProxyGenAssembly2")] is present in the test assembly.

`8-24`: **Fix invalid method group assignment test case.**

The new invalid test data provides good coverage of edge cases, but one test case has incorrect syntax.



The test case on line 19 has invalid syntax:
```csharp
["""mockProvider.SetupAdd(x => {|CS0131:x.ToString()|} += It.IsAny<Action>());"""]
```

The issue is that `ToString()` is a method, not an event, so `x.ToString() +=` is not valid syntax for event operations. This should be testing assignment to an actual event.

Apply this diff to fix the test case:

```diff
-            // Method group assignment, compiler emits CS0131 (not assignable)
-            ["""mockProvider.SetupAdd(x => {|CS0131:x.ToString()|} += It.IsAny<Action>());"""],
+            // Invalid event assignment syntax, compiler emits CS0131 
+            ["""mockProvider.SetupAdd(x => {|CS0131:x.StringEvent|} = It.IsAny<Action<string>>());"""],
```

This tests assignment (`=`) instead of subscription (`+=`) which would trigger CS0131.

**📄 CodeRabbit Inference Engine (CONTRIBUTING.md)**

> `tests/**/*.cs`: Run all unit tests using 'dotnet test --settings ./build/targets/tests/test.runsettings'. All tests must pass.
> Every analyzer and fixer must have comprehensive unit tests.
> Use the project's data-driven test pattern for code fix tests: provide a public static IEnumerable<object[]> data source, use [Theory] and [MemberData], and define a static Template function with placeholders.
> In code fix tests, the code that triggers the analyzer MUST be wrapped with the diagnostic marker syntax (e.g., {|Moq1210:...|}).
> Use AllAnalyzersVerifier for tests that verify code patterns don't trigger unwanted diagnostics from any Moq analyzer.
> Group analyzer tests by Moq version compatibility: place tests for features only available in Moq 4.18.4+ in a 'new' group, and tests for features available in both 4.8.2 and 4.18.4 in a 'both' or 'old' group.
> Do not include tests for APIs or patterns that do not exist in the targeted Moq version; such tests will fail at compile time.
> Only set up or verify virtual, abstract, or interface members in Moq analyzer tests. Do not attempt to set up or verify non-virtual, static, or sealed members.
> Use SetupAdd and SetupRemove only for virtual events, and only in Moq 4.18.4+.
> Setups for explicit interface implementations must use the correct cast syntax (e.g., ((IMyInterface)x).Method()).
> Use .Protected().Setup(...) only for protected virtual members, and only in Moq 4.18.4+.
> Do not add analyzer tests for static, const, or readonly members, as Moq cannot mock or set up these members.
> Always include both Task and ValueTask scenarios in analyzer tests for async methods.
> If your analyzer or code fix interacts with Callback or SetupSequence, ensure you have tests for both single and sequence setups.
> Always specify and test for both MockBehavior.Default and MockBehavior.Strict where relevant in analyzer tests.
> If mocking internal members, ensure [assembly: InternalsVisibleTo("DynamicProxyGenAssembly2")] is present in the test assembly.

# Review Notes for PR 937

## Findings
- **Major** — `src/Dock.Avalonia/Internal/DockHelpers.cs:138`: caught exceptions now route through `DockLogger.Log`, which exits early when `DockSettings.EnableDiagnosticsLogging` is false; this regresses the previous always-on diagnostics and hides hit-test failures.
- **Major** — `src/Dock.Settings/DockLogger.cs:29`: `[Conditional("DEBUG")]` on `LogDebug` strips the calls from release builds, so the new diagnostics toggles cannot emit logs when investigating issues outside debug configurations.
- **Medium** — `src/Dock.Avalonia/Internal/DockHelpers.cs:48`: `LogDropAreas` walks the entire visual tree even when diagnostics logging is disabled; guard the method early to avoid the extra per-move overhead.
- **Minor** — `src/Dock.Model/FactoryBase.cs:207`: duplicated `CopyDockGroup(dockable, split);` invocation (with odd indentation) causes the copy to run twice; remove the extra call.

@copilot , the former codes review brought following observations:

💡 Codex Review
https://github.com/piotr-gorczynski/Soccer/blob/da43e5322e144517a8c53e99449a4489092de691/mobile/app/src/main/java/piotr_gorczynski/soccer2/InvitationsActivity.java#L299-L309
P1 Badge Preserve loaded pages when snapshot listener updates

The real‑time listener rebuilds the pending invitations list with pendingAdapter.setData(...) every time Firestore pushes an update. After the user taps Load more, any new invite or status change will trigger this callback and replace the adapter’s contents with only the first page (10 items), discarding the pages that were manually appended. The same pattern is used for past invites. This effectively breaks pagination as soon as the data changes while the screen is open.

https://github.com/piotr-gorczynski/Soccer/blob/da43e5322e144517a8c53e99449a4489092de691/mobile/app/src/main/java/piotr_gorczynski/soccer2/InvitationsActivity.java#L312-L315
P1 Badge Pagination cursor derived after filtering out expired invites

When calculating lastPendingDoc and hasMorePendingInvites you rely on pendingInvites, which has been filtered to remove expired entries and then sorted by createdAt. The Firestore query is ordered by expireAt, so the last element of this filtered/sorted list may not correspond to the last document returned by the query, and its size may be < page size even when more unexpired invites exist after the first page. In scenarios where the first 10 results contain expired items, the load‑more button will never appear and startAfter(lastPendingDoc) can skip or repeat invitations. The pagination cursor and has‑more flag should be derived from the raw query snapshot or by filtering server‑side.
Please add snapshot test for this template.

Step-by-Step Instructions
Identify the Service Account: The service account for your Firebase Functions is typically in one of these two formats:

PROJECT_ID@appspot.gserviceaccount.com (The default App Engine/Cloud Functions service account)
service-PROJECT_NUMBER@gcf-admin-robot.iam.gserviceaccount.com (Less common for new projects, but possible)
The most common one for default Cloud Functions is PROJECT_ID@appspot.gserviceaccount.com. Replace PROJECT_ID with your actual project ID (soccer-dev-1744877837).

Go to the Google Cloud IAM Page:

Open the Google Cloud Console.
Ensure your project (soccer-dev-1744877837) is selected.
Navigate to IAM & Admin -> IAM (or search for "IAM" in the console).
Find and Edit the Service Account:

Look for the service account identified in Step 1 (e.g., soccer-dev-1744877837@appspot.gserviceaccount.com) in the list of principals.
Click the pencil icon (Edit principal) next to that service account.
Add the Required Role:

Click + Add another role.
In the "Select a role" dropdown, search for "Vertex AI User" (roles/aiplatform.user). This role contains the necessary aiplatform.endpoints.predict permission12.
Select the role and click Save.
